event: The Garden of Eden with animals
symbols: white horse — nobility and purity of untamed creation before the Fall
peacock — immortality and resurrection in early Christian symbolism, the incorruptible beauty of paradise
lion coexisting with prey — the peaceable kingdom where predator and prey dwell together (Isaiah 11:6-7)
diverse animal species gathered — the fullness of God's creative act on the fifth and sixth days
lush green landscape — the fertile perfection of Eden before the curse upon the ground
birds in flight — freedom and the soaring spirit of creation under God's blessing
description: A lush landscape teeming with diverse animals gathered together in apparent harmony. At left, a white horse with a red saddle blanket stands prominently. A rooster, hen, and turkey occupy the lower foreground. A peacock displays near the center. A lion with open mouth stands beside a spotted dog. A large deer with antlers appears at right. Birds of prey soar overhead. The background shows green trees, a distant building, and soft blue sky with clouds. Smaller animals are scattered throughout the grassy field.
theological_description: This Edenic animal scene evokes the primordial harmony of Genesis 1-2, when God created every beast of the field and bird of the air and pronounced them good. The peaceful coexistence of lion, deer, horse, and domestic fowl in a single landscape recalls the prophetic vision of Isaiah 11:6-9, where the wolf dwells with the lamb and the lion eats straw like the ox. This is both a backward glance at Eden before the Fall and a forward vision of the messianic restoration. The absence of human figures focuses attention on creation itself as the subject of divine delight. Early Church Fathers like Basil of Caesarea (Hexaemeron) saw in the diversity of animal creation a reflection of God's infinite wisdom and generosity. The peacock, a widespread early Christian symbol of resurrection and immortality, anchors the composition in eschatological hope — the restoration of all things (Acts 3:21).

scripture_refs.reason: God creates every living creature — livestock, creeping things, and beasts of the earth according to their kinds
God brings every beast of the field and bird of the air to Adam — the original naming and dominion in Eden
The peaceable kingdom where predator and prey coexist — the eschatological restoration of Edenic harmony
The restoration of all things spoken by the prophets — paradise regained in the messianic age
O Lord, how manifold are your works — the earth is full of your creatures, celebrating the diversity depicted
